summary: Spolsky responds to a critic who argued that his 'eat your own dog food' philosophy is a barrier to proper usability testing. He clarifies that dogfooding and usability testing are not mutually exclusive, pointing out that he explicitly includes hallway usability testing in The Joel Test and devotes a chapter to it in his book. He dismisses the critique as a logical fallacy, noting that advocating for one practice doesn't mean opposing another.
key_points: Dogfooding and usability testing are complementary practices, not competing ones
Hallway usability testing is already one of the 12 requirements of The Joel Test
The critic's argument commits a logical fallacy by treating two compatible practices as mutually exclusive
Microsoft, cited as a counter-example, actually does both dogfooding and extensive usability testing
money_quotes: Just because I said 'eat your own dog food' doesn't mean I don't advocate usability testing. It's just a logical fallacy.
They could have said 'Eat Your Own Dog Food, but Not If It Means The Dog Will Go Hungry.' It just doesn't follow.
Sorry, you can't read it, he works for Giga and they charge for their ideas.
key_insight: Advocating for dogfooding does not imply opposing usability testing — the two practices are complementary, and treating them as mutually exclusive is a logical fallacy.
